behavior: It cannot cross an unbroken line of ash, so it stalks mourners until they track soot into a gap; then it "repairs" the line behind them by dragging its abdomen like a chalk stick, sealing the room and taking its time.

It only attacks after being invited—so it imitates a loved one’s knock pattern perfectly, and if you refuse twice it leaves a tiny wooden latch as a gift; the third night, that latch appears already installed on your door from the inside.

It wants warmth but refuses fire; it presses its muzzle to sleeping chests to borrow body-heat, and if you wake it by touching it, it becomes obedient for an hour—then follows you for days, waiting to be "dismissed" by being ignored.

It is drawn to vows spoken aloud; it hovers near mouths during promises and feeds on the vibration, and once it has eaten your "yes," it returns later to harvest the regret as a low, private ringing only you can hear.

It never enters a home; it stands outside in the rain reciting your minor sins in a gentle, helpful tone, and each time you answer back—deny, justify, confess—it grows more articulate, until it can speak as you in your own voice.

It is obsessed with neatness of fate; it follows groups and "corrects" coincidences by snipping threads it alone can see, causing small accidents to happen in the right order, and it becomes furious if anyone says, "We got lucky."

It wants to balance accounts of waste; it appears whenever food is thrown away and offers a silent inventory with claw-taps, and if you refuse to eat what you discard, it begins replacing your fresh meals with exact, cold replicas of what you wasted.

It does not chase bodies, only reflections; it rearranges mirrored surfaces to trap a person’s image in a cornered angle, and once your reflection cannot "exit" a mirror normally, you start forgetting where your own hands are unless you’re being watched.

It is compelled to "tend" the injured, but only by making the wound symmetrical; it waits until someone is hurt, then softly tries to match the damage on the other side with careful, loving precision, humming as if soothing a child.

It wants your attention to be portable; it offers a lantern that shows exactly what you fear behind every door, and if you keep it, you become unable to look away from small light sources—candles, screens, streetlamps—until it can carry your gaze off in its jar.
